title: Check out the Aulumu A17 Rugged case on the Cosmic Orange iPhone 17 Pro!

There is a little bit of play on the sides and the top and that's because this is all one piece and there's no hard plastic reinforcing the case. In my opinion, this is just fine. One other downside is that this case has no soft lining at all on the inside. So any dust or sand that gets between the case and the phone has a chance of scratching up your phone. If you get this case, make sure you take it off and clean the inside regularly. All of the cutouts are precise and in the right places, which we would expect now from a reputable case manufacturer. The USB cutout is kind of big, so little chance of blocking your charging cable. And as you can see, the speaker cutouts are like big huge cutouts, not individual holes. And I think I prefer this style. Now we come to the second worst thing about this case, the button. Now, I understand what Alumo was trying to do here. They were trying to give a rugged case some rugged buttons. And I do appreciate the chunkiness of the buttons and how they can easily be felt when holding the case, so you always know where they are. And this is important because as iPhones get more and more and more buttons, it becomes more and more difficult to differentiate the locations. The only thing is on the left side, it's kind of hard to tell the difference between the volume buttons and the action button. But my biggest gripe is that in order to make this button style work, they had to leave huge holes on the sides of the buttons where dust and lint are going to get into the case and probably scratch your phone. I know they only wanted to do one molding for this case, but they did some extra around the camera control and managed to seal that off. I just wish they did the same for the other buttons as well. Looking at extra features now. There aren't any really except for this lanyard hole where you can attach one if you want. And this is especially useful if you also have the G09 kickstand for mounting to steel surfaces like gym equipment. Fireworks. One thing that I like to know about cases is how your hand feels after a long day of use. This A7 rugged ranks highly on my daily comfort scale because of its soft TPU with a fine matte surface which makes it pleasant to hold and the super rounded corners help with this feeling. I also have not tried rugged cases for the iPhone 17 Pro from other brands because generally I strongly dislike rugged cases, but the fact that I can stand this one does say something. So to conclude, overall I think this is a solid rugged case. It totally wins in the look department. Nothing nothing else even comes close. I am however disappointed in the buttons. Look, I think this case has a lot of style and protection, but I do think it would look best with the silver or blue iPhone. I think the little bit of orange showing through is just the wrong amount and kind of clashes with the case a bit too much. For the orange iPhone 17 Pro, I actually would have preferred if the case was solid black, but then that would have been a totally different look as well because you wouldn't see the like translucency around the edges here when you hold it up against the light. So, eh, also, if the buttons were sealed, this would have been an easy recommendation. But with the gaps there like that, I think you're going to have to take the phone out and clean the inside of the case regularly. So, that's it, guys. I do recommend this case with some caveats. Remember, although Alum did send me this sample for feedback, this is an unsponsored and unaffiliated review. So, if you appreciate this kind of content, sponsor me by liking and subscribing, or even better yet, leave a comment below and tell me what you think about this case. So, that's it, guys. title: Every iPhone Camera Compared: The Shocking Evolution!
transcript: ﻿It’s been almost 20 years since Steve Jobs  introduced the first iPhone. That one didn’t   have a selfie camera, couldn’t record videos, and  only had a tiny 2MP rear camera. Fast forward to   now — the iPhone 17 has two cameras, with the  main lens at 48MP — that’s 24 times more pixels   than the original — and a much larger sensor, plus  massively improved computational power. If you put   photos from the first-generation iPhone next to  the latest one, the difference is unbelievable.   We’ve come a long way in the past two decades,  but the pictures from the original iPhone still   have their own charm. When I was comparing all  generations of iPhones just out of curiosity, I   realized that progress was a bumpy road — in some  cases, it actually felt like image quality went   backwards. We’ll get into what I mean later, but  to keep the timeline consistent, let’s talk about   the iPhone 3G next! The iPhone 3G was introduced  a year and a half after the first iPhone, in   June 2008. This version had integrated GPS and 3G  support, but from the camera perspective, it was   identical to the original iPhone — still a 2MP  shooter without a selfie camera and without video   recording. Maybe that’s why if we put the original  iPhone and iPhone 3G photos next to each other,   you can’t really tell the difference. But hey, at  least it has a nostalgic aura to it. Then Apple   released the iPhone 4 in 2010 and the iPhone  5 in 2012. The iPhone 5 had an 8MP camera,   which is a lot more than the original iPhone.  They also went back to the aluminum body,   added the Lightning port, and included a selfie  camera — this generation already looks and feels   like a modern device. I should also say I really  like how the iPhone 5 pictures look. The optical   quality is much better than the original iPhone,  but due to the lack of computational photography,   the photos look soft — in a good way. Yes, details  are lacking, but this is the kind of image a   photographer like Maria would rather get. Also,  this iPhone had video capabilities. The video is   nothing groundbreaking — you can feel the exposure  shifting and the stabilization isn’t great — but   it has a nice, soft, nostalgic look to it. The  iPhone 6 came out in 2014 with faster focusing,   but the iPhone 7 was when things started to  change. In 2016, Apple introduced the iPhone   7 with a 12MP camera and a larger sensor,  and the iPhone 7 Plus was the first phone   to feature a dual-camera setup. It was also  the year that Harambe were killed! They also   added a lot more computational photography  to their photos, this is a hot take — but   I definitely prefer the iPhone 5 pictures over  the iPhone 7. When it comes to video, though,   there’s a clear upgrade from the iPhone 5 — the  colors are more accurate, and Maria’s face pops   more. The iPhone 8 came out a year later in  2017 in two variants: the base iPhone 8 with   a single-camera setup and the iPhone 8 Plus with a  main camera and a telephoto lens. The main camera   on the iPhone 8 was the same as the iPhone 7’s  but added 4K 60FPS video functionality and better   color sensors. The pictures from the iPhone 7  and iPhone 8 look almost identical — sharpened,   contrasty, and with muted colors on both phones.  After the iPhone 8, Apple released the iPhone X,   XS, and XR in 2017 and 2018. These devices had  edge-to-edge screens, Face ID, and looked like   modern iPhones. Touch ID was now gone. The iPhone  XR had a larger 1/2.55" sensor and a 12MP camera.   They also switched to a 26mm focal length, which  is wider, and have kept this focal length to this   day on the base iPhone models. If we look at the  pictures from the iPhone 8 side by side with the   iPhone XR, the XR produces more accurate colors  — it’s more vibrant, and the white balance leans   toward magenta instead of green, giving more life  to Maria’s face. The same can be said about the   video — the only thing is that I find the iPhone 8  handles exposure better, while the iPhone XR tends   to overexpose the image a bit. One year later, in  2019, the iPhone 11 was released — and this phone   is actually my current work phone. The iPhone 11  had a dual-camera setup, marking the first time   a base model had two cameras. The second camera  was an ultrawide lens. The iPhone 11 also had a   12MP main camera with the same sensor size as the  iPhone XR, but with a newer sensor. We can see a   slight color shift compared to the iPhone XR — the  iPhone 11 is more vibrant and slightly brighter.   Personally, I low-key prefer the iPhone XR here.  If we compare the iPhone 11 to the iPhone XR in   terms of video, the iPhone 11 seems to have a  greener white balance, but overall the quality is   quite comparable. The next phones were the iPhone  12 and 13, which also had a cute mini version, but   the next one I want to talk about is the iPhone  14 — released in 2022 with a much larger 1/1.7"   sensor, though still only 12MP on the main camera.  It’s no surprise that compared to the iPhone 11,   the iPhone 14 photos have more bokeh and appear  sharper — maybe even a bit too sharp, depending on   your taste. I’ll let you judge that for yourself.  I’d say that, for video, the quality is in the   same league — it seems the video pipeline is  nearly identical between the iPhone 11 and 14.   The iPhone 14 just has a bit more bokeh. Before  we go to the iPhone 15, please take a moment to   subscribe to the channel if you enjoy this kind of  content. We’re trying to reach 80K subscribers by   the end of this month — right now, we’re at  70K! In 2023, Apple released the iPhone 15,   which featured a larger 1/1.56″ sensor and, for  the first time, a 48MP main camera instead of   12MP. That’s why, compared to the iPhone 14, you  can see a big jump in photo quality — skin tones   are more accurate, and the bokeh looks better,  giving the photos a more professional feel. In   2024, Apple released the iPhone 16 with a better  processor and introduced the new camera button in   this generation. However, the main camera remained  the same, so it’s no surprise that if you compare   the iPhone 15 and 16 side by side, you’ll notice  almost no difference — maybe just a slight white   balance shift, which could simply be due to the  lighting angle. Then, this year — just a few   months ago — we got the iPhone 17. This phone has  a few notable upgrades over the last generation,   like the new 120Hz display and the square selfie  camera. But when it comes to the main camera,   the sensor size and pixel count remain the same.  That’s why photos from the main camera are almost   identical to those from the iPhone 16 — which,  in turn, looked very similar to the iPhone 15.   The video quality is also more or less the same;  personally, I can’t really tell the difference   between the two. Now, if we zoom out and look  from the first-generation iPhone to the iPhone 17,   I’d say there’s a clear improvement from the  original iPhone up to the iPhone 5. Then, Apple   started to compensate for the lack of optical  quality with heavy computational photography — a   trend that continued until the recent iPhones,  where we’re finally seeing a return to more   natural and less contrasty photos. And of course,  the camera isn’t just about the main lens and   portrait shots. Apple has improved video quality,  secondary cameras, autofocus, night photography,   and many other aspects generation by generation.  But it also feels like we’re gradually reaching a   plateau as the iPhone matures. There are also now  Pro versions with better telephoto cameras. If you   enjoyed this video, you can watch my comparison  of the latest iPhone 17 versus the iPhone 16 Pro   to see how those two stack up. Thanks for tuning  in to Techmo, and I’ll see you in the next one!

title: Stop iCloud Photo Sync on iPhone 17 Pro Max [How to Turn Off]
transcript: Hey, is your iPhone automatically uploading all your photos and videos to iCloud? So, let's see how you can turn off iCloud photo sync on your iPhone 17 Pro Max or any other iPhone running with iOS 26. All right. So, the first thing you have to do go to your settings then from your Apple account go to iCloud and now from save to iCloud section you will find iCloud photos. Just go for it. And now you will see the sync this iPhone option. So if it's turned on that means your photo and videos are automatically syncing to iCloud. So now to turn off iCloud photo syncing just toggle that switch off. So from here you will see two option remove from iPhone and download photos and videos. [music] So if you choose remove from iPhone your photo will be deleted from your iPhone but will still remain safely stored in iCloud. But if you select download photos and videos, all your photos and videos from iCloud will be downloaded and kept on your iPhone as well. So I go for remote from iPhone. And that's it. That's how you can turn off iCloud photo sync on your iPhone. Hope this guide helped. Thanks for watching.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max Review: The Ultimate Pro iPhone Experience
transcript: The Apple iPhone 17 Pro Max pushes the iconic iPhone in bold new directions and colors. It's recognizable but different in ways that make it eye-catching. The performance is stellar and the cameras set a new high watermark for smartphone photography. For people who demand more from their smartphone, there may be no better choice. If you are looking different in your iPhone shopping journey, the iPhone 7 Pro Max has you covered. It's not just that fetching cosmic orange, the unexpected hue heat of Apple's outdropping event. Apple's iPhone 7 Pro Max features a fresh design with new materials, and a broad and bold product spanning camera plateau that adds a distinctive flare to what had become a somewhat tired design. Inside the changes are no less significant. From the powerful A19 Pro chip to the new paper chamber supported heat management system which enable enough performance to support every peak and valley of your experience. If you are fixated on cameras, the iPhone 7 Pro Max will not disappoint. There are now finally three 48 MP lenses that support a wide range of shooting styles. Perhaps the most notable of the three is the new 48 megapixel 4x optical zoom lens that also offers a access to a sensor crop 8x zoom which thanks to selecting the center pixels from the larger sensor and an upgraded image pipeline delivers some truly eyepopping photos. This is also an upgrade that makes a canny swap of titanium for aluminium a seeming downgrade, but one with some significant benefits like the ability to apply that amazing new anodized orange finish and the ability to better distribute and dispate heat. Finally, there's the price. It's the one thing you hope doesn't get an upgrade. And I am happy to report that Apple somehow held the line here. The iPhone 7 Pro Max still starts at $1,199. It's never been a cheap smartphone, but then this one is for the pros, and I think they will be very happy. I know I am. As for why you might buy the iPhone 7 Pro Max over the iPhone 7 Pro, that comes down to screen size, battery life potential, and the option to get up to 2 TB of storage. Otherwise, these iPhones are identical. The iPhone 7 Pro Max was announced at Apple's dropping event on September 9. Pre-orders begin on September 12th and the phone arrives in stores and starts shipping on September 19. The iPhone 7 Pro starts at $1,199 for the model with 256GB of storage with that price rising to $1,399 for 512GB of storage, $1,599 for 1 TB storage, and $1,999 for 2 TB of storage. The later configuration represents the largest storage capacity of any iPhone ever. Apple is likely getting a new credit for not raising the price of the base model iPhone 7 Pro Max. Component prices and supply chain issues relating to the geopolitical stays are surely putting pressure on the company. Somehow too, Apple has held the line and the base iPhone 7 Pro Max still starts at $1,199 for 256GB model. The only difference in pricing comes into play when you stretch to the phone's new upper tier of 2 TB, which is only available with the Pro Max model. That sends the price up to almost $2,000. It boggles the mind that we now consider paying almost 2 grand for a pocket-sized device to be rational. On the other hand, this is a true prolevel smartphone that based on my tests is probably ready for pro photography and videography tasks. And when you put it like that, it might seem like a bargain. I saw an in the runup to the iPhone 7 line launch to have a pretty good idea of what was coming. And to be honest, I was preparing to hate the iPhone 7 Pro Max redesign and colors. Yet, here I am now quite pleased with the giant plateau. and yes, loving cosmic orange. In many ways, the iPhone 7 Pro Max is still undoubtly part of long lineage of iPhones before it. There are though just enough new touches here to add up to what I am comfortable calling a redesign. Starting with the back, there's the now nearly full width plate that's both a bold design touch, but also a practical measure. in that underneath there's now more space for upgraded components like refreshed tetra prism that supports a new longer telephoto lens. Most previous iPhones featured just one material usually metal or glass. The iPhone 7 Pro Max is a spiffy mix of the two metal and glass or rather a larger rectangular ceramic shield cutout with the rest a univity chassis literally carved out of aluminium. Instead of a titanium band surrounding a frame components and the front and back glass, the iPhone 7 Pro Max's back cascades seamlessly to the side and even curves just a bit around the front to meet the ceramic shield 2 screen covers which reportedly will better protect it from scratches. It will has an incredibly unified feel and because Apple has radically cut down on ages, the phone feel very comfortable to hold. When it comes to dimensions and weight, the iPhone 7 Pro Max is slightly larger and heavier than the iPhone 16 Pro Max, but I challenge anyone to notice the differences which can be measured in fraction. The iPhone 7 Pro Max is 163 mm tall by 78 mm long by 8.75 mm thick and weighs 233 g. By contrast, the iPhone 16 Pro Max was 163x 77.6x 8.25 25 mm and weighted 227 g. This year there are no new buttons to contend with. Along one age we have the long power and C button and below it camera control. Opposite them is the action button and below that a pair of volume buttons. There are small differences along the shorter edges. On top, an antenna cutout is now visible and on the bottom, the pair of speaker grills appear larger. If you want to lie the phone flat, you'll have to place it screen down. Even though the back plateau is nearly the full width of the phone, the tri camera array still pumps out even further. And between this and the wide metal bump, this phone lies on its back at a slightly more extreme angle than the iPhone 16 Pro Max. The switch from titanium to aluminium provides not only some useful heat management and energy efficiency benefits, it's also opened the door for anodized color options. I don't know where Apple got the idea for cosmic orange, but I actually love it. It's bold without being garish. There's also silver and the very deep and inky deep blue. Apple sort of explained its color choices to me and even the loss of black, but I think this is just Apple mixing things up and possibly giving a nod to all the prolevel folks who buy this phone for creative pursuits. Orange is a color that will get you noticed. iOS 26's liquid gloss interface might lead you to assume there has been some radical display overhaul, so much glow, shiny, and artificial transparency. However, that would be mostly wrong. The perform update does change the look, but it's all still working with the same materials. The iPhone 7 Pro Max's expensive 6.9 in super retina XDR OLED is largely the same as last year's featuring a resolution of 2868 by320 pixels for a density of 460 ppi. The prootion technology still automatically ranges from 1 Hz to 120 Hz and it's always on which means that even when you are in bed you can make out the time and have lensible notifications. It's not though exactly the same screen. This display now ranges up to 3,000 nits. That's 1,000 more than the last model. In my sideby-side tests, the iPhone 7 Pro Max is more visible in direct sunlight. This effect is assisted by a new reflected coating, meaning the light bouncing of the screen is also less noticeable. There are some nice and not necessarily insignificant upgrades. With a narrow bezel, the super retina display comes precisely close to meeting that orange metal with a vast expanse of color and entertainment interrupted only by the peel-shaped dynamic island. made up of the new center stage camera and Face ID sensors. It remains a useful space for live information, but it's also sometimes a black blob cut out in games, videos, and photos. I find the information it provides useful, so I do not mind it much, but I was also hoping for a redesign that might have shrunk the thing by 50%. This is a minor quel and I'm sure that like me, you probably won't notice or be bothered by it very much. Apple's redesigned plateau is not just an aesthetic choice, it's practical one. Underneath that now larger raised platform is a lot of technology, including a new tetroprism to support Apple's longest and highest resolution telephoto lens ever. It's simply one highlight of a stellar iPhone 7 Pro Max camera system. There are a total of four cameras with Apple calling the rear trio cameras. The main camera is 48 MP, ultrawide is also 48 MP and telephoto also 48 MP with 4x optical and the selfie camera is 18 MP. Those specs only tell half the story. In general, you are not shooting at the full-frame 48 megapixel resolution. Or rather, you might be using all 48 million pixels to produce a high quality 24 megapixel or even 12 megapixel to produce an 8x optical quality sensor crop. The ultrawide will by default shoot 12 megapixel macroos. In virtually every instance, this is a case where less is more. Apple uses all that pixel information and its remarkable image pipeline to deliver fantastic photos with true life colors and exquisite detail. Here are some camera samples of the iPhone 17 Pro Max. Few things contributes to the new look and feel of the iPhone 17 lineup as much as iOS 26. Liquid gloss adds a translucent sheen to almost every aspect of the platform and mostly it's gorgeous. Apple has done an excellent job of programming pixels to look like glass. Sometimes too the transparency is overdone and I struggle to make out some interface elements. Think of it this way. When you can see through one element to view another, it can occasionally increase the overall clutter and make some things visually confusing. For example, when you swipe down on the screen to access global search, the search box still faintly shows what's behind it, which makes what's on top of it. What you are typing a little harder to read. It's a small issues that Apple could easily address in the next iOS update. In some cases too, like the new firstparty app icons, control center, and dock, iOS 26 is a welcome update that gives everything a little polish without throwing out the most recognizable elements. Apple Intelligence gets a few updates like live translation and the ability for images captured through visual intelligence to be transformed into calendar entries. I played a bit with Genoji and Image Playground to experience those upgrades, but they are mostly minor and I still await the fully featured Siri that Apple has promised. The iPhone 7 Pro Max is a stellar flagship phone based on my testing. You get a more powerful zoom camera, better sustained performance, and the longest iPhone battery life ever, a head turning design. The iPhone 7 Pro Max is still behind the competition on AI, and it's a bit thick and heavy, but overall, it is easily one of the best big screen phones yet.
